content.text: Standard chunking splits documents into fixed-size or semantically-bounded windows. Each chunk is indexed and retrieved independently. This creates a fundamental problem: a chunk that reads perfectly in the context of the full document becomes ambiguous or meaningless in isolation.

Consider a 200-page financial report. A chunk from the R&D section might read: "It increased 22% to accelerate the company's AI capabilities." Without knowing which document this came from, which section, or what "it" refers to, this chunk will fail to retrieve for any query about R&D spending. The chunk is factually correct but informationally incomplete.

(Anthropic, 2024) solves this by prepending a short LLM-generated context summary to each chunk before indexing. The summary situates the chunk within the document — restoring the referential context that naive splitting destroys.

attrs.alt: Contextual Retrieval diagram. Left: naive chunking produces a chunk with 'It increased 22%' — the pronoun loses its referent. Right: LLM prepends 'Context: CloudCorp Q3 2025 earnings, R&D investment section.' to the chunk before indexing. Pipeline: chunk → LLM context generation (with prompt caching) → dense embed + BM25 index → RRF fusion → answer. Bottom: cost model (95% reduction with prompt caching) and Anthropic's benchmark (failure rate cut from 5.7% to 1.9% with full stack).

content.text: The LLM receives the full document and the individual chunk, and produces a 1–3 sentence description that situates the chunk within the document. This description is prepended to the chunk text before embedding and BM25 indexing:

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

CONTEXT_PROMPT = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("human",
     "<document>\n{full_document}\n</document>\n\n"
     "Here is the chunk we want to situate within the whole document:\n"
     "<chunk>\n{chunk_text}\n</chunk>\n\n"
     "Please give a short succinct context (1–3 sentences) to situate this chunk "
     "within the overall document for the purposes of improving search retrieval. "
     "Answer only with the succinct context and nothing else."),
])

# Claude 3 Haiku is fast and cheap for this task
llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", temperature=0)
context_chain = CONTEXT_PROMPT | llm | StrOutputParser()

full_doc = """CloudCorp Q3 2025 Earnings Report
Revenue: Cloud revenue grew 34% YoY to $2.1B.
Enterprise: Added 2,400 new logos.
R&D: Investments increased 22% to accelerate AI.
CEO: Expects continued growth in Q4."""

chunk = "It increased 22% to accelerate the company's AI capabilities."

context = context_chain.invoke({
    "full_document": full_doc,
    "chunk_text": chunk,
})
print(context)
# "This chunk is from CloudCorp's Q3 2025 earnings report, specifically from
#  the R&D investment section, discussing the 22% increase in R&D spending."
Prepending the context to the chunk

def contextualise_chunk(chunk_text: str, context: str) -> str:
    """Combine the generated context with the original chunk for indexing."""
    return f"{context}\n\n{chunk_text}"

contextualised = contextualise_chunk(chunk, context)
print(contextualised)
# "This chunk is from CloudCorp's Q3 2025 earnings report, specifically from
#  the R&D investment section, discussing the 22% increase in R&D spending.
#
#  It increased 22% to accelerate the company's AI capabilities."
Without caching, contextualising every chunk independently sends the full document to the LLM once per chunk — extremely expensive for long documents. With

, you send the document once and cache its KV representation. Each subsequent chunk call reuses the cached document prefix, paying only for the short suffix (the chunk + instruction text) plus the output tokens.
(from ~$23 to ~$1.02 per million tokens of document context) when processing chunks from the same document in a single batched session with prompt caching enabled.
Batched contextualisation with Claude prompt caching

marker tells the API to cache everything up to and including that content block. On the first chunk, both system prompt and document are cached. From the second chunk onward, only the output and short per-chunk suffix are billed at full price.

After contextualisation, index the combined text (context + chunk) in both a dense vector store and a BM25 index. Both retrievers benefit: BM25 gains the correct entity names and section labels; the dense embedder encodes a richer, more situated representation.

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
import anthropic

# ---- Step 1: load and chunk the document ----
full_document = open("cloudcorp_q3_2025.txt").read()

def simple_chunk(text: str, chunk_size: int = 500, overlap: int = 50) -> list[str]:
    """Naive word-count chunker for illustration."""
    words = text.split()
    chunks, start = [], 0
    while start < len(words):
        end = min(start + chunk_size, len(words))
        chunks.append(" ".join(words[start:end]))
        start += chunk_size - overlap
    return chunks

raw_chunks = simple_chunk(full_document)

# ---- Step 2: contextualise all chunks ----
contextualised = contextualise_document_chunks(full_document, raw_chunks)

# ---- Step 3: build dense index ----
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
langchain_docs = [
    Document(
        page_content=c.combined_text,
        metadata={"original": c.original_text, "context": c.context, "chunk_idx": i},
    )
    for i, c in enumerate(contextualised)
]
vectorstore = FAISS.from_documents(langchain_docs, embeddings)
vectorstore.save_local("contextual_index")

# ---- Step 4: build BM25 index ----
tokenised_chunks = [c.combined_text.lower().split() for c in contextualised]
bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenised_chunks)
print(f"Indexed {len(contextualised)} contextualised chunks.")

At query time, run both retrievers against the contextualised index, fuse with RRF, and optionally rerank. The pipeline is identical to standard hybrid retrieval — contextualisation is purely an indexing-time concern:

from collections import defaultdict
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

reranker = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2", max_length=512)

def rrf(ranked_lists: list[list[int]], k: int = 60) -> list[tuple[int, float]]:
    scores: dict[int, float] = defaultdict(float)
    for ranked in ranked_lists:
        for rank, idx in enumerate(ranked, start=1):
            scores[idx] += 1.0 / (k + rank)
    return sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

def contextual_retrieve(
    query: str,
    k_initial: int = 20,
    k_final: int = 5,
) -> list[Document]:
    # Dense retrieval
    dense_docs = vectorstore.similarity_search(query, k=k_initial)
    dense_indices = [d.metadata["chunk_idx"] for d in dense_docs]

    # BM25 retrieval
    bm25_scores = bm25.get_scores(query.lower().split())
    bm25_indices = sorted(range(len(contextualised)), key=lambda i: bm25_scores[i], reverse=True)[:k_initial]

    # RRF fusion
    fused = rrf([dense_indices, bm25_indices])
    candidate_indices = [idx for idx, _ in fused[:k_initial]]

    # Fetch docs for reranking
    candidate_texts = [contextualised[i].combined_text for i in candidate_indices]
    rerank_scores   = reranker.predict([(query, text) for text in candidate_texts])
    ranked          = sorted(zip(rerank_scores, candidate_indices), reverse=True)

    # Return top-k with original chunk text (not the contextualised version)
    results = []
    for _, idx in ranked[:k_final]:
        c = contextualised[idx]
        results.append(Document(
            page_content=c.original_text,   # return clean chunk to LLM
            metadata={"context": c.context, "chunk_idx": idx},
        ))
    return results

docs = contextual_retrieve("What was Q3 R&D spending?")
for doc in docs:
    print(f"  Context: {doc.metadata['context'][:60]}")
    print(f"  Chunk:   {doc.page_content[:70]}")
    print()
text (context + chunk) to the retrievers for better matching, but pass only the
to the LLM for answer generation. The context blurb is a retrieval aid, not document content — including it in the LLM prompt wastes tokens and can confuse the model.
For large document corpora, contextualising chunks synchronously is slow. Async calls with concurrency control keep the pipeline fast without exceeding API rate limits:

import asyncio
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ContextualisedChunk:
    original_text: str
    context:       str
    combined_text: str

async def contextualise_chunk_async(
    client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic,
    full_document: str,
    chunk_text: str,
    model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    semaphore: asyncio.Semaphore = None,
) -> ContextualisedChunk:
    async with semaphore:
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=200,
            system=[{
                "type": "text",
                "text": "You generate short retrieval contexts. Reply with 1–3 sentences only.",
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
            }],
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": [
                    {
                        "type": "text",
                        "text": f"<document>\n{full_document}\n</document>\n",
                        "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
                    },
                    {
                        "type": "text",
                        "text": f"Situate this chunk:\n<chunk>\n{chunk_text}\n</chunk>",
                    },
                ],
            }],
        )
    context = response.content[0].text.strip()
    return ContextualisedChunk(
        original_text=chunk_text,
        context=context,
        combined_text=f"{context}\n\n{chunk_text}",
    )

async def contextualise_all(
    full_document: str,
    chunks: list[str],
    concurrency: int = 10,
    model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
) -> list[ContextualisedChunk]:
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)
    tasks = [
        contextualise_chunk_async(client, full_document, chunk, model, sem)
        for chunk in chunks
    ]
    return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

# Usage
contextualised = asyncio.run(contextualise_all(full_document, raw_chunks, concurrency=10))
print(f"Contextualised {len(contextualised)} chunks")
Structured docs with clear section headings

when LLM contextualisation is too expensive, extract the document title, section heading, and nearest named entity using regex or a lightweight NER model, and prepend that as a structured prefix. It is far cheaper and still substantially improves retrieval over no context at all.
For corpora where documents have consistent structure (e.g. earnings reports, legal filings, technical manuals), a template-based approach avoids LLM cost entirely:

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from langchain_core.documents import Document

@dataclass
class DocumentMetadata:
    title:   str
    section: str
    date:    str | None = None
    entity:  str | None = None

def extract_metadata(full_document: str) -> DocumentMetadata:
    """Extract structured metadata from a financial document."""
    # Title: first non-empty line
    title = next(
        (line.strip() for line in full_document.splitlines() if line.strip()),
        "Unknown Document",
    )
    # Section: look for lines ending with ":" or all-caps headings
    section_pattern = re.compile(r'^[A-Z][\w\s&]+:?$', re.MULTILINE)
    sections = section_pattern.findall(full_document)
    section = sections[0].rstrip(":") if sections else "General"

    date_match = re.search(r'Q[1-4]\s+\d{4}', full_document)
    date = date_match.group(0) if date_match else None

    return DocumentMetadata(title=title, section=section, date=date)

def rule_based_context(
    chunk_text: str,
    meta: DocumentMetadata,
    chunk_idx: int,
    total_chunks: int,
) -> str:
    parts = [f"From: {meta.title}"]
    if meta.date:
        parts.append(f"Period: {meta.date}")
    if meta.section:
        parts.append(f"Section: {meta.section}")
    parts.append(f"Chunk {chunk_idx + 1} of {total_chunks}.")
    return " | ".join(parts)

# Usage
meta = extract_metadata(full_document)
for i, chunk in enumerate(raw_chunks):
    ctx = rule_based_context(chunk, meta, i, len(raw_chunks))
    combined = f"{ctx}\n\n{chunk}"
    print(f"Chunk {i}: {combined[:120]}")
